attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: A decolonial retelling of Alice in Wonderland as poetry. Alice travels to the Amazon in 2025 and falls asleep by the Rio Negro. The traditional spirits of the forest speak to her — Curupira, Iara, Boto, Saci-Pererê, Boitatá, Caipora. The Queen of Hearts becomes the Queen of Extraction: spirits of capital that wear humans like gloves to further their goals. Inspired by Lewis Carroll's structure but rooted in Amazonian cosmology, indigenous resistance, and the ongoing destruction of the rainforest.

attribution.name: Lewis Carroll
attribution.note: Original structure and characters from Alice's Adventures in Wonderland (public domain). This retelling transforms, not translates — the Amazon is not a metaphor for Wonderland. Wonderland was always the Amazon, dreaming.

name: Alice na Amazônia / Alice in the Amazon: The Forest Speaks in Verse
description:
A decolonial retelling of Alice in Wonderland as poetry. Alice is a climate researcher who travels to the Amazon in 2025. She falls asleep by the Rio Negro and enters the forest's dream — where Curupira guards the roots, Iara sings the river's memory, Boto shapeshifts between worlds, and the Queen of Extraction plays croquet with human bodies. Each poem follows Carroll's chapter structure but replaces English nonsense with Amazonian cosmology: the forest is not wonderland — it is the original mind, older than language, and it has something to say to the girl who came to measure it. Written entirely in verse.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Amazonian botanical illustrations from Alfred Russel Wallace's 'A Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro' (1853). Illustrations from Henry Walter Bates' 'The Naturalist on the River Amazons' (1863). Ernst Haeckel's 'Kunstformen der Natur' (1904) — particularly jungle flora plates. Brazilian folk art woodcuts (xilogravura) from cordel literature tradition.

items.name: Canto I — Into the Forest / Para Dentro da Floresta

sections.Verse:
Alice came to measure trees.
She brought her instruments, her grants, her good intentions,
her notebook full of numbers that would save the world
if only the world were made of numbers.

She sat beside the Rio Negro,
black water that refuses to reflect —
the river that swallows light
the way the forest swallows roads.

Her eyelids fell like leaves.

And the forest,
which had been waiting for someone
who would fall asleep with their mouth open,
began to speak.

Not in Portuguese. Not in Tupi.
Not in any language that has a word for 'resource.'
In the tongue that roots use
when they decide which way to grow.

Alice fell
not down a hole
but into the soil —
past the leaf litter and the beetle highways,
past the mycelium that connects
every tree to every other tree
like a conspiracy of kindness,
past the bones of those who came before
with their instruments and grants and good intentions
and were composted
into something the forest could use.

She fell until falling
became a kind of growing.
Downward. Rootward.
Into the original dark
where everything begins.
sections.The Spirit: The forest itself. No single entity — the dreaming of 10 million species thinking together. The mycorrhizal network that Paul Stamets calls 'Earth's natural internet' — except the forest calls it nothing, because naming is a human problem.
sections.Decolonial Note: Alice arrives as researcher — the latest in a long line of Europeans who came to the Amazon to classify, measure, extract, and explain. Wallace, Bates, Humboldt, Spruce — brilliant men who saw clearly and still couldn't see. The forest's first move is to put her to sleep. Not violence. Not rejection. Just: stop talking. Start listening. The descent mirrors ayahuasca ceremony: you don't take the vine to the laboratory. The vine takes you to its laboratory.

items.name: Canto II — The River Rises / O Rio Sobe

sections.Verse:
Alice woke in water.
Not drowning — breathing it,
the way fish breathe,
the way the forest breathes
when the river rises twenty meters in the wet season
and the trees become islands
and the dolphins swim between the canopy.

She was crying
but couldn't tell her tears from the river.
'My tears are freshwater,' she said,
'and the river is freshwater,
so how will I know which sadness is mine?'

A voice from below:
'You won't. That's the point.'

Iara surfaced —
half woman, half current,
hair like black water weeds,
eyes like two moons
drowned in their own reflection.

'You're the one who measures,' Iara said.
'Tell me: how many liters of grief
are in the Rio Negro today?
How many parts per million of memory?
What is the pH of a mother
who watched her village flood
because someone in São Paulo
needed another parking lot?'

Alice opened her notebook.
The pages were wet.
The numbers had dissolved
into small fish
that swam away
before she could count them.

'Every river is made of tears,' said Iara.
'The Amazon carries the tears of the Andes.
The Negro carries the tears of the forest.
Your tears carry the tears of a girl
who thought she could save something
without first learning
how to grieve it.'

Alice closed the notebook.
The small fish circled back,
curious now
that she had stopped trying
to catch them.
sections.The Spirit: Iara — the water spirit of Brazilian folklore. Part siren, part guardian, she lives where the river is deepest. In colonial tellings, she drowns men who desire her. In the older stories, she is the river's consciousness — she doesn't drown anyone. She simply shows them how deep the water goes, and some cannot bear it.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Pool of Tears is absurdist — Alice cries so much she nearly drowns in her own tears. Here the tears are real: climate grief, ecological mourning, the particular sadness of measuring a dying system with instruments designed by the system that is killing it. Iara's question — 'how many liters of grief?' — is not mockery. It's an invitation to stop quantifying and start feeling. The dissolved numbers becoming fish: data returning to life.

items.name: Canto III — The Circle of Animals / O Círculo dos Bichos

sections.Verse:
On the bank sat animals
that should not have been sitting:
a jaguar cleaning its teeth with a twig,
a harpy eagle folding its wings like a newspaper,
a capybara the size of a Volkswagen
(or perhaps a Volkswagen the size of a capybara —
in the forest, the reference points shift),
and a sloth who had been sitting there
since the Pleistocene
and saw no reason to rush the introduction.

'We were having a race,' said the jaguar,
'but we couldn't agree on a finish line.'

'In the old world,' said Alice,
'everybody runs in a circle
and everyone wins a prize.'

The animals looked at each other.

'That's not a race,' said the capybara.
'That's an ecosystem.'

Then Curupira came —
small, red-haired, feet on backwards,
leaving footprints that lead
wherever you aren't going.

'I am the guardian,' he said,
'of everything that doesn't want to be found.
The trees that hide from the chainsaws.
The rivers that change course overnight.
The animals that go extinct
and then un-extinct themselves
when no one is looking.'

'Can things un-extinct themselves?' asked Alice.

'Only if you stop looking for them
with the eyes that made them extinct.
Try looking with your feet.
But put them on backwards first.'

Alice tried.
She fell.
The animals laughed —
not at her,
but with the forest,
which laughs the way rain laughs:
by falling on everything equally.
sections.The Spirit: Curupira — the forest guardian of Tupi-Guarani cosmology. A small, fiery-haired being whose feet face backwards, so anyone who tracks him follows the prints in the wrong direction. He protects animals and trees from hunters and loggers. His backwards feet are not a defect — they are epistemology. To find what matters, stop going forward.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Caucus Race is political satire — everyone runs, nobody wins, everyone gets prizes. The decolonial flip: the animals aren't racing because competition is a colonial import. An ecosystem doesn't have winners. The capybara's correction — 'That's not a race, that's an ecosystem' — is the grammar's thesis in miniature. Curupira's backwards feet: you cannot find the forest by going forward. Progress is the wrong direction.

items.name: Canto IV — The Rubber Baron's House / A Casa do Barão da Borracha

sections.Verse:
In the middle of the forest:
a mansion.
Tiled in Portuguese blue,
chandeliers from Paris,
opera house in the garden —
because when you have stolen enough rubber
you can build Versailles in the jungle
and the jungle will spend a hundred years
taking it back.

The walls were cracking.
Orchids grew from the piano.
A tree had pushed through the ballroom floor
with the patience of a revolution
that knows it has more time
than the thing it's replacing.

Saci-Pererê sat on the banister —
one leg, red cap, pipe in his mouth,
grinning the grin of someone
who has watched empires rot
for five hundred years
and never stops finding it funny.

'Welcome to the house of progress,' he said.
'They built it with latex and blood.
The rubber tappers bled the trees
and the foremen bled the tappers
and the investors in London
bled the foremen by telegram.
Everyone was bleeding something.
They called it commerce.'

Alice touched the wall.
It was warm. Breathing.

'The house is alive?' she asked.

'The house is dying,' said Saci.
'The forest is alive.
The house just happens to be
where the forest is doing
its most beautiful dying.
Look —'

He pointed his pipe
at the chandelier,
where a family of bats
had replaced the candles
with their own small fires:
eyes that see in the dark,
which is the only kind of seeing
the house was built to prevent.

'Every empire builds a house,' said Saci.
'And every house becomes a ruin.
And every ruin becomes a garden.
The only question is:
how long do you insist
on living in the house
after the garden
has already started?'
sections.The Spirit: Saci-Pererê — the most beloved trickster in Brazilian folklore. A one-legged Black boy with a red cap and a pipe, born from Tupi-Guarani and Afro-Brazilian traditions. He causes mischief: knots in horse manes, soured milk, lost objects. But his chaos is diagnostic — he breaks what deserves breaking. His one leg is not disability; it is efficiency. He needs only one foot to kick down a rotten door.
sections.Decolonial Note: The Rubber Baron's house is real. Manaus has the Teatro Amazonas, an opera house built in 1896 with rubber money — Italian marble, French chandeliers, English steel — while indigenous and caboclo rubber tappers were enslaved in debt peonage. The house that Alice enters is every colonial monument: beautiful, built on suffering, and being slowly reclaimed by what it tried to replace. Saci's question — how long do you live in the house after the garden has started? — is the question for every institution.

items.name: Canto V — The Vine Speaks / A Vinha Fala

sections.Verse:
On a branch that was also a question
sat a vine that was also an answer.

It did not smoke a hookah.
It did not need to.
It was its own smoke.

'Who are you?' asked the vine.

Alice had been asked this before,
in a book she'd read as a child,
and the answer had been clever and circular
and ultimately about a little English girl
who couldn't remember her multiplication tables.

But here,
in the forest's green dark,
the question was not philosophical.
It was botanical.

'Who are you?
What are your roots?
How deep?
How wide?
What do you feed
and what feeds you?
What dies when you die?
What lives because you live?
If I pulled you up by your roots,
what would come with you —
what soil, what fungi,
what thousand invisible threads
that connect you
to things you've never seen
but cannot live without?'

Alice began to answer
with her name, her university,
her funding body, her research question,
her hypothesis about carbon sequestration
in secondary growth forests —

'No,' said the vine.
'Those are your labels.
I asked who you are.
A label is what you put on a thing
after you've killed it
and pinned it to a board.
I'm asking about the living thing
before the pin.'

Alice was quiet.

The vine waited.
Vines are good at waiting.
They have been climbing
since before there were trees to climb,
and they will be climbing
after the last tree
has been converted
into the last IKEA shelf.

'I don't know,' said Alice.

'Good,' said the vine.
'Now we can begin.
The ones who know who they are
never stay long enough
to learn.'
sections.The Spirit: The ayahuasca vine — Banisteriopsis caapi. Not the DMT-containing leaf (Psychotria viridis) that Westerners fixate on, but the vine itself, which indigenous peoples call 'the teacher.' In Amazonian cosmology, ayahuasca is not a drug — it is a being, a plant-teacher that has been in relationship with human communities for thousands of years. The vine doesn't give answers. It dissolves the self that was asking the wrong questions.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Caterpillar asks 'Who are you?' as a riddle about identity and size. The vine asks it as an ecological question: what are you connected to? What dies when you die? This reframes identity from individual to relational — you are not your name, your institution, your funding body. You are your roots, your mycelium, your interdependencies. The vine's rejection of labels ('a label is what you put on a thing after you've killed it') is a direct challenge to Linnaean taxonomy — the colonial project of naming and classifying the world as a precondition for owning it.

items.name: Canto VI — The Fire and the Smoke / O Fogo e a Fumaça

sections.Verse:
The forest was burning.
Not metaphorically.
Not in a poem.
The forest was burning
the way forests burn in 2025:
set alight by men
who need the land
for soybeans
that will feed cattle
that will feed cities
that will feed the feeling
of never being full.

Alice ran.

But in the smoke
something moved —
not away from the fire
but through it,
coiling like a river made of light,
eyes like two embers
that remember being stars.

Boitatá.
The fire serpent.
Guardian of the fields.

'Do not run,' it said.
'You cannot outrun a fire
that you helped set.'

'I didn't set this fire!' said Alice.

'Do you eat?' asked Boitatá.
'Do you buy? Do you sell?
Do you live in a city
that was once a forest?
Do you wear clothes
made from cotton
grown where jaguars
used to teach their cubs to hunt?
Then you set this fire.
Not with a match.
With a fork.'

The fire roared.
Alice felt its heat
not on her skin
but in her chest —
the particular burning
of knowing something
you've always known
but kept in a drawer
labeled 'later.'

'I am the fire that protects,' said Boitatá.
'I burn the eyes of those
who destroy the forest at night,
when they think no one is watching.
But now the fires are so large
that I cannot tell
my fire from theirs.
They have stolen my element.
They burn with it
and call it development.'

The smoke cleared.
The forest was not gone.
But it was thinner,
the way a person is thinner
after a long illness —
still standing,
but you can see the bones.
sections.The Spirit: Boitatá — the fire serpent of Tupi-Guarani mythology. A giant serpent made of fire that protects the forests and fields. It blinds those who set illegal fires. In some tellings, it is the spirit of a great serpent that survived a primordial flood by eating only the eyes of dead animals, becoming luminous. It is fire that serves life — the opposite of the fires that serve capital.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's chapter with the Duchess, the cook, and the pepper is about domestic chaos — too much spice, a baby that turns into a pig. Here the chaos is real: the Amazon fires of 2019-2025, set deliberately to clear land for agribusiness. Boitatá's accusation — 'you set this fire with a fork' — traces the supply chain from the dinner table to the burning forest. The fire serpent's crisis is that extractive capitalism has stolen fire itself, turning a sacred, protective element into a weapon of clearing.

items.name: Canto VII — The Night of the Spirits / A Noite dos Encantados

sections.Verse:
At a table made of a fallen tree
that was not dead but resting,
three spirits sat
drinking from cups
that refilled themselves
from the rain.

Caipora — wild-haired, wild-eyed,
riding a peccary backwards,
smelling of wet earth
and the inside of a storm.

Boto — in his human form tonight,
white suit, white hat,
handsome as a lie,
one nostril slightly wrong
(the blowhole he can never
quite hide).

Mapinguari — enormous,
fur like moss, mouth in its belly,
one eye in the center of its forehead
that sees only the truth,
which is why it keeps it closed.

'You're late,' said Caipora.
'You're early,' said Boto.
'You're exactly on time,' said Mapinguari,
'which means the clock is broken,
which is the only way
a clock can tell the truth.'

Alice sat down.
There was no clean cup.

'Move down!' said Caipora.
They all moved one seat to the left.
Nothing changed
except everything.

'In your world,' said Boto,
smoothing his white suit,
'time moves forward.
Monday, Tuesday, profit, progress,
deadline, bottom line, finish line.
Always a line. Always forward.
Always toward something
that turns out to be
another line.'

'Here,' said Caipora,
'time moves in circles.
Wet season, dry season.
Bloom, fruit, rot, bloom.
The river rises. The river falls.
The river rises. Nothing is wasted.
Nothing is late.
Nothing is early.
Everything is composting
into the next thing.'

'Your world is terrified of rot,' said Mapinguari,
opening his belly-mouth to smile.
'You wrap your food in plastic
to stop it from dying.
You wrap your dead in formaldehyde
to stop them from becoming soil.
You wrap your grief in therapy
to stop it from becoming wisdom.

You are the only species
that fights decomposition.
And you wonder
why nothing in your world
can grow.'

Alice looked at her tea.
It was not tea.
It was rainwater
that had fallen through
fifty meters of canopy,
touching ten thousand leaves
on the way down,
each leaf adding
a small green syllable
to the taste.

She drank.
It tasted like time
moving in circles.
sections.The Spirit: Three spirits together — Caipora (forest guardian, rides a peccary, controls animals), Boto (the pink river dolphin who shapeshifts into a handsome man to attend parties — the blowhole he can never hide is his tell), and Mapinguari (a giant one-eyed being covered in matted fur with a mouth in its belly, possibly the spirit-memory of the giant ground sloth that once lived in the Amazon). Together they represent the forest's relationship with time: cyclical, composting, anti-linear.
sections.Decolonial Note: The Mad Tea Party's joke is that time has stopped — it's always six o'clock, always teatime. Carroll's time-stop is whimsical; the forest's time-stop is cosmological. Linear time — Monday-Tuesday-profit-progress — is the temporal infrastructure of colonialism. You need linear time to have 'progress,' and you need 'progress' to justify extraction. The forest's circular time (wet/dry, bloom/rot/bloom) is not primitive — it is the only temporal framework that doesn't require infinite growth on a finite planet. Mapinguari's critique of anti-decomposition culture is Vanessa Andreotti at her sharpest.

items.name: Canto VIII — The Extraction Game / O Jogo da Extração

sections.Verse:
The garden was perfect.
Too perfect.
Every tree in rows.
Every row productive.
Every product measured,
weighed, and sold
before it was even ripe.

And at the center,
on a throne made of shipping containers,
sat the Queen of Extraction.

She was not a person.
She was a system
wearing people.

Her crown was made of quarterly reports.
Her scepter was a futures contract.
Her robes were sewn
from the flags of companies
that don't exist anymore
because they were acquired
by larger companies
that don't exist anymore
because they merged
with the concept of money itself.

'Who is this?' said the Queen,
pointing at Alice
with a hand that was
simultaneously
a CEO in São Paulo,
a lobbyist in Brasília,
a trader in Chicago,
and a algorithm in a server farm
that runs on electricity
generated by a dam
that drowned a river
that Iara used to sing in.

'I'm Alice. I'm a researcher —'

'PERFECT,' said the Queen.
'We love researchers.
Researchers measure the forest
so we know how much is left.
Researchers write papers
that delay action
by exactly one funding cycle.
Researchers produce knowledge
that we convert to intellectual property
that we license back to them
at a rate that requires
more grants
that require
more measurement
that delays
more action.
It's the most elegant game.
We call it PROGRESS.'

She clapped her hands.

The game began.
It was croquet,
but the mallets were people
and the balls were other people
and the hoops were policies
that kept moving
just far enough
that you could never quite
pass through them.

'OFF WITH THEIR TREES!' screamed the Queen,
and a section of forest
became a spreadsheet
became a stock price
became a bonus
became a yacht
became a tax write-off
became a charitable foundation
that funded research
into why the forest was disappearing.

Alice dropped her mallet.
The mallet said 'thank you'
and walked away
on human legs.
sections.The Spirit: The Queen of Extraction — not a person but a system that wears people. She is capital itself: the logic of endless growth that uses human bodies as instruments. CEOs, lobbyists, traders, algorithms — they are not the Queen. They are her hands. She picks them up when useful and discards them when spent. She is the extractive spirit that Carroll's Red Queen only hinted at: the force that demands you run faster and faster just to stay in the same place, because the place itself is being sold.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Queen of Hearts is absurd tyranny — 'Off with their heads!' is meaningless because no one is ever actually beheaded. The Queen of Extraction is effective tyranny — 'Off with their trees!' is meaningful because every tree IS actually felled. The croquet game with human mallets: in extractive capitalism, people are instruments. The 'elegant game' of research-funding-delay is real — the production of knowledge as a substitute for action, measurement as a form of consumption. Alice dropping the mallet and the mallet walking away is the moment of recognition: the tool was a person.

items.name: Canto IX — The Boto's Lament / O Lamento do Boto

sections.Verse:
After the game,
Alice found Boto
sitting alone by the river,
his white suit stained with mud,
his human shape flickering
like a candle in wind.

'I used to be beautiful,' he said.
'I would come to the festivals
in my white suit, my white hat,
and the girls would dance with me
all night long.
In the morning I'd slip back
into the river,
pink and grinning,
and they'd find a wet spot on the dance floor
and know I'd been there.'

'What happened?' asked Alice.

'Mercury,' said Boto.
'The gold miners use it
to separate gold from river mud.
It settles in the water.
It settles in the fish.
The fish settle in me.
Now my brain is cloudy.
I forget which shape I am.
Sometimes I surface
and I'm not sure
if I'm a dolphin dreaming of a man
or a man dreaming of a dolphin
or mercury dreaming of both.

The river used to be
my dance floor.
Now it's my pharmacy.
Every breath is a dose.
Every fish is a pill
I didn't choose to take.'

He looked at Alice
with eyes that were
slowly forgetting
what they were for.

'The Mock Turtle in your story
mourned an education he never had.
I mourn a river I still have
but can no longer trust.
Which is worse —
to lose a thing
or to have a thing
that is slowly
becoming
its own poison?'

A fish jumped.
Boto watched it
with hunger
and with fear —
the two feelings
that mercury
had made
indistinguishable.
sections.The Spirit: Boto — the pink river dolphin (Inia geoffrensis), the Amazon's most beloved shapeshifter. In folklore, he transforms into a handsome man in a white suit to attend river festivals, seducing women and returning to the water at dawn. He is charm, desire, and the river's erotic life. Mercury poisoning from illegal gold mining (garimpo) is destroying the species — and with it, one of the Amazon's oldest love stories.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Mock Turtle weeps for a classical education he never received — a satire of Victorian schooling. Boto weeps for a river he still has but can no longer trust. The mercury poisoning is literal: illegal gold mining (garimpo) has contaminated Amazon rivers, poisoning dolphins, fish, and the indigenous communities who eat them. Boto's confusion between his shapes — dolphin/man/mercury — mirrors the bioaccumulation: the poison doesn't replace you. It becomes you. You can't separate yourself from what you've swallowed.

items.name: Canto X — The Dance of the Waters / A Dança das Águas

sections.Verse:
Iara returned,
but not alone.
With her came the rivers:

The Amazon,
wide as a country,
carrying more water
than the next seven rivers combined,
having nothing to prove.

The Negro,
black as sleep,
acidic, tea-colored,
so low in nutrients
that mosquitoes can't breed in it —
the river that protects you
by being inhospitable
to the things that would eat you.

The Solimões,
muddy, fertile,
carrying the Andes in suspension,
every glass of it
a geology lesson.

Where the Negro meets the Solimões
they do not mix.
For six kilometers
black water and brown water
flow side by side,
refusing to become one thing.

'This is the dance,' said Iara.
'Not merging. Not conquering.
Not one river swallowing the other.
Two rivers
running together
without becoming the same.

Your world calls this a problem.
Diversity. Pluralism.
How do we make them mix?
How do we make them one?

The rivers call it Tuesday.
They've been doing it
for eleven million years.
They don't need a policy.
They need to be left alone.'

The rivers danced.
Not a waltz — waltzes have leaders.
Not a quadrille — quadrilles have steps.
Something older:
the movement water makes
when it remembers
that it was once rain
and will be rain again
and in between
it gets to be a river,
which is the most beautiful thing
water knows how to do.

Alice watched
and understood
that she was mostly water
watching water
dance with water
about water.

She had never felt
less alone.
sections.The Spirit: The rivers themselves — in Amazonian cosmology, rivers are not resources or transport routes. They are beings. The Meeting of the Waters (Encontro das Águas) near Manaus, where the Rio Negro and Rio Solimões flow side by side without mixing for six kilometers, is one of Earth's most visible demonstrations that difference does not require dissolution.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Lobster Quadrille is a dance with strict steps — a parody of Victorian social choreography. The Dance of the Waters has no steps, no leader, no choreography. The Meeting of the Waters is the Amazon's natural refutation of the colonial melting pot: two rivers can flow together without becoming one. This is not diversity as policy — it's diversity as physics. Iara's 'your world calls this a problem / the rivers call it Tuesday' is the simplest decolonial move: what colonialism frames as a problem to solve, the forest experiences as reality functioning normally.

items.name: Canto XI — Who Sold the Forest / Quem Vendeu a Floresta

sections.Verse:
The trial was held
in a clearing
that used to be a forest.

The judge was a harpy eagle
who had nowhere left to perch
and so perched on the law itself,
which was the only tall thing remaining.

The jury: twelve stumps.
Each one a witness.
Each one silent
in the way that only
something that used to be alive
can be silent.

'Who sold the forest?'
asked the eagle.

A long pause.

Then everyone spoke at once:

'The logger sold it to the rancher.'
'The rancher sold it to the soy trader.'
'The soy trader sold it to the commodity market.'
'The commodity market sold it to the futures index.'
'The futures index sold it to a pension fund in Norway.'
'The pension fund sold it to the concept of retirement security.'
'Retirement security sold it to the fear of dying without enough.'
'The fear of dying without enough
sold it to the original wound:
the belief that you are separate
from what you consume.'

The eagle blinked.

'So the forest was sold
by everyone to everyone
through the medium of fear?
And the purchase price
was the forest itself?'

'Yes, Your Honor.'

'And what remains?'

Curupira stood.
'The roots remain, Your Honor.
They go deeper than the chainsaws.
They go deeper than the law.
They go deeper than the fear.
Someone planted them
before there were people
to be afraid,
and they will be here
after the people
have finished
being afraid.'

The stumps said nothing.
But if you pressed your ear
to any one of them
you could hear
a slow, deep hum —
the sound of roots
still connected
underground,
still passing sugar
and warnings
and something that,
if you translated it
from tree,
would sound like
'hold on.'

'Hold on.
Hold on.
Hold on.'
sections.The Spirit: All the spirits convene — this is the forest's trial. The harpy eagle (the Amazon's apex predator, now endangered) serves as judge. The jury of stumps: in Amazonian indigenous jurisprudence, the land itself has standing. The stumps are not metaphors — they are plaintiffs.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's trial is about who stole the tarts — a trivial crime adjudicated with maximum pomposity. Here the crime is real and the chain of responsibility is traced from the physical (logger) through the financial (commodity market, pension fund) to the psychological (fear of scarcity, the wound of separation). The Norwegian pension fund is not fiction — Norway's Government Pension Fund has been linked to Amazon deforestation through its investments. The trial reveals that no single villain sold the forest: a system of abstraction made it possible for everyone to participate in destruction without anyone holding the knife. The roots saying 'hold on' — both 'persist' and 'wait, stop.'

items.name: Canto XII — Alice Wakes / Alice Acorda

sections.Verse:
The Queen of Extraction screamed:
'OFF WITH HER FUNDING!'

And the cards flew up —
but they were not cards.
They were leaves.
Thousands of leaves,
falling and rising,
falling and rising,
the way leaves do
when the forest breathes out
and the wind is just right
and for one moment
the air is more green than transparent.

Alice opened her eyes.

She was lying by the Rio Negro.
Her notebook was open.
Her pen had rolled into the water.
A small pink dolphin
surfaced, looked at her,
and dove.

The forest was quiet.
The kind of quiet
that is not the absence of sound
but the presence
of ten thousand things
choosing not to speak
at the same time,
which is the most sophisticated
form of music.

Alice looked at her notebook.
The pages were dry.
The numbers were intact.
The hypothesis about carbon sequestration
in secondary growth forests
was still there,
waiting to be tested.

But next to the numbers,
in handwriting that was hers
but also not hers —
loopier, greener,
as if written by someone
who had roots instead of fingers —
were words she didn't remember writing:

'The forest is not a carbon sink.
The forest is not a resource.
The forest is not a laboratory.
The forest is not a metaphor.
The forest is not yours to save.

The forest is a mind
that has been thinking
for fifty-five million years
and has not yet
reached a conclusion
because a mind this old
knows that conclusions
are where thinking
goes to die.

You came to measure.
Stay to listen.
The data you need
is not in the numbers.
It is in the silence
between the numbers —
the part your instruments
were designed
not to hear.'

Alice closed the notebook.
She did not leave.

She sat by the river
and listened
to the space
between the measurements,

where something was growing
that had no name
and needed none
and would outlast
every name
ever given to it

including
this one.
sections.The Spirit: The forest releasing Alice — but not unchanged. In ayahuasca ceremony, the return is the most important part: what do you bring back? What has composted? Alice doesn't leave. She doesn't go back to publish. She sits and listens. The notebook with writing she doesn't remember — the vine's handwriting, root-writing — is the grammar itself: words that come from somewhere older than the person holding the pen.
sections.Decolonial Note: Carroll's Alice wakes and it was all a dream — Wonderland collapses into a child's nap. This Alice wakes and it is NOT a dream — the forest is real, the destruction is real, the spirits are real in the way that matters: they are the forest's intelligence, expressed through the only language humans can hear, which is story. Alice's choice to stay and listen rather than return with data is the decolonial turn: from extraction (even of knowledge) to relationship. The final lines — 'something that had no name and needed none and would outlast every name ever given to it including this one' — is the grammar's own self-erasure. Even this poem is a label. The forest is the thing before the pin.

items.name: The Descent — Into the Forest's Dream

sections.About: The opening movement: Alice arrives as a researcher, falls asleep, and enters the forest's dreaming. She descends through the soil like a root, and surfaces in the river's tears. The two cantos trace the passage from measurement to feeling — from notebook to water, from numbers to grief. The forest's first teaching: you cannot save what you have not learned to mourn.
sections.For Readers: Read these two cantos together as a single breath: inhale (the descent into soil) and exhale (the surfacing in water). Alice's instruments dissolve. Her data becomes fish. This is not loss — it is translation. The forest is converting her from one language to another.

items.name: The Forest Speaks — The Spirits Teach

sections.About: The central movement: five spirits teach Alice five lessons. Curupira teaches that finding requires going backwards. Saci teaches that every empire becomes a garden. The vine teaches that identity is connection, not label. Boitatá teaches that complicity travels through supply chains. The night spirits teach that circular time is the only time compatible with life. Each lesson dismantles one pillar of colonial thinking: progress, property, identity, innocence, and linear time.
sections.For Readers: These five cantos can be read in any order — the forest doesn't lecture sequentially. Each spirit offers one lens. Together they compose a way of seeing that is incompatible with extraction: if your feet face backwards, if your house is a ruin becoming a garden, if your identity is your roots, if your dinner plate connects to fire, if time is a circle — then 'progress' becomes meaningless. That's the point.

items.name: The Queen — Extraction Wears a Human Face

sections.About: The antagonist movement: the Queen of Extraction is revealed as a system, not a person — capital wearing human hands. Her croquet game uses people as mallets. Then Boto shows the cost: mercury poisoning, shapeshifting gone wrong, a river you can no longer trust. And the rivers respond with the dance that needs no choreographer: difference without dissolution, coexistence without merging. The movement traces destruction → grief → an alternative already in motion.
sections.For Readers: The Queen is the hardest canto because she is not evil — she is efficient. She doesn't hate the forest; she can't see it. She sees resources, commodities, futures contracts. Boto's lament is what efficiency looks like from inside the river. The Dance of the Waters is what was always already here, before efficiency arrived and after it leaves.

items.name: The Trial — Who Sold the Forest, and the Waking

sections.About: The closing movement: the trial reveals that no single villain sold the forest — a chain of abstraction (logger → rancher → trader → market → pension fund → fear) made everyone complicit and no one responsible. Then Alice wakes — but unlike Carroll's Alice, she does not return to the 'real world' dismissing the dream. She stays. She listens. The notebook has writing she doesn't remember: the forest wrote back. The grammar ends with its own dissolution — 'something that had no name and needed none.'
sections.For Readers: The trial is not about blame. It's about tracing the invisible threads that connect a pension fund in Oslo to a stump in Rondônia. The waking is not about answers. It's about the choice to stay in the question. Alice's notebook with the vine's handwriting: this is what decolonial education looks like. Not the teacher filling the student, but the student finding that something has already been written in them, in a handwriting that is theirs but also not theirs.

items.name: The Forest's Dream — A Decolonial Alice in Twelve Cantos

sections.About: The complete arc: a young researcher arrives in the Amazon to measure carbon, falls asleep, and is taught by the forest's spirits that measurement is itself a form of extraction — that the instruments designed to save the forest were designed by the same worldview that is destroying it. The twelve cantos follow Carroll's Alice chapter by chapter but replace English nonsense with Amazonian cosmology, Victorian absurdism with climate grief, and the comfortable ending ('it was all a dream') with an uncomfortable one ('the dream is the only real thing here'). The grammar's thesis: Wonderland was always the Amazon. The madness was always colonialism. The Queen was always capital. And Alice — the curious, measuring, categorizing, well-meaning European girl — was always us.
sections.Lineages: Feeds directly into: Vanessa Andreotti (hospicing modernity, the house that is becoming a garden), Bayo Akomolafe (the world is not a problem to be solved), Josh Shrei (animist revival, plant intelligence, sacred ecology). Also in conversation with: Ailton Krenak (Ideas to Postpone the End of the World), Davi Kopenawa (The Falling Sky), Eduardo Viveiros de Castro (Amerindian perspectivism), Anna Tsing (The Mushroom at the End of the World).
